results.possibleIdentity: Irving S. Rosner was a prominent New York-based electrical engineer and television systems specialist active from the early 1960s through the 1990s. He was the founder and president of Rosner Television Systems, Inc., a firm located in Manhattan (with offices at 250 West 57th Street) that specialized in turnkey installations, engineering consultation, and mobile vehicle construction for the television and film industries. Rosner's expertise spanned electronics, television, film, and the then-emerging field of cable television (CATV). In 1971, he served on the Mayor's Task Force on CATV in New York City, contributing technical expertise to the city's early cable policy. He was a Fellow of the Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers (SMPTE) and a frequent contributor to its technical programs, serving as a topic chairman and moderating panels on subjects such as computer-aided design and high-definition television (HDTV). His publications include the co-authored 1974 paper 'A Portable Compact Color Television Facility' and the 1991 article 'The Changing Role of the Television Engineer.' Rosner was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) engineering registry, which facilitated collaborations between technical experts and artists in New York during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was married to Risha Fraiberg in 1963 and was a long-time resident of Great Neck and Plainview, New York. He passed away prior to 2010.
